summary: Jeff Atwood argues against the overuse of code folding directives like C#'s #region, which he considers harmful to code quality and maintainability. While acknowledging the importance of adapting to team coding standards, he makes an exception for this practice, which he views as enabling developers to hide poorly written code. He presents four main criticisms: regions are meaningless to compilers, they encourage hiding bad code, they mask excessive method length, and they compensate for editor deficiencies. His core message is that well-written code should be inherently readable without needing organizational crutches.
key_points: Code folding directives like #region are glorified comments that add lines to accommodate the editor rather than improve the actual code
Folding enables developers to sweep ugly or boilerplate code under the rug, where it rots unseen rather than getting properly refactored
The presence of folded code creates a false sense of organization while masking spaghetti code and excessive method length
Standard boilerplate regions represent editor deficiencies that should be handled automatically, not manually by developers
When joining a team, developers should generally adapt to existing practices, but some preferences warrant early discussion
money_quotes: I can't see anything! I have to manually expand those sections to browse any of the code in this class.
Code that hides from you is code that will rot in the most putrescent and painful way possible.
I daresay being able to see the damn code is more important than having it meticulously segmented into six pointless little named buckets.
Your code should be front and center at all times – exposed to as many programmers' eyes, and as much healing light, as possible.
If the code needs the crutch of folding to look organized, it's bad code.
Why, exactly, are we writing code to accommodate the editor?
I've always been wary of cowboy coders who rolled into an ongoing project on fresh horses and immediately started dictating terms.
key_insight: Well-written code should be inherently readable and organized without requiring editor features to hide its complexity; if you need code folding to make your code presentable, that's a sign you need to refactor, not fold.
